summary: A manager discovers that a senior engineer has secretly spent two weeks rewriting a core system in an entirely new language instead of adding the requested feature. Fournier argues that while engineer initiative should be encouraged, there's legitimate overhead to adding new technologies that warrants process. She recommends having clear policies for introducing new languages/frameworks, requiring advocacy, learning commitments, and production readiness plans. When violations occur, managers should first understand the engineer's perspective—they may reveal communication failures on both sides. The conversation should then clarify expectations around scope communication and socializing changes with the team.
key_points: Engineer initiative is valuable, but adding new languages/frameworks has real overhead that justifies some process around adoption
A good policy requires advocates to present benefits, create production standards, and have multiple engineers commit to learning the new technology
When someone goes rogue, first ask why—you might discover you're unapproachable or that they feared being shot down
Senior engineers are expected to push information to managers and socialize changes with their team, not work in isolation
Part of seniority is knowing when scope changes require communication, not just technical competence
money_quotes: The last thing you want to do is stifle people's initiative to create solid solutions and learn new things.
There is a high overhead to adding new things to your stack, and at some point, it usually makes sense to have some policy around how to add new things.
Part of being a senior engineer is knowing when to communicate scope changes such as the need to move to new languages or frameworks.

Perhaps you skip all of their 1-1s and they think you are unapproachable. Perhaps they are frustrated with the way you make decisions. Perhaps they knew you would be mad and were simply afraid to show you new work early when you would shoot it down.
Socializing change not only to your manager but to your team is part of the role of the senior engineer.
All too often teams will reject projects they weren't aware of and didn't have any say in.
key_insight: When an engineer goes rogue, the problem is rarely just about the engineer—investigate whether your own approachability or decision-making created conditions where they felt hiding work was the safer path.
